Details:
Reduction of perchlorate by different biological treatment methods can be very effective (exceeding 99 percent and to below detection limits of 4 ppb or lower) under anaerobic and is regulated by presence of oxygen, molybdate, and to some extent nitrate. Biological perchlorate reduction technologies such as fluidized bed reactor, biofilm reactor, fixed film reactor and suspended growth reactor uses different filter media with perchlorate-reducing bacteria to reduce perchlorate from the drinking water. Other biological treatment methods that do not include biological filtration such as microbial reduction of perchlorate with zero-valent iron, bioreactors filled with iron fillings or glass beads also falls in this treatment category.
Microbial reduction is attractive because the hazardous perchlorate is converted to two innocuous compounds, chloride and oxygen. Acetate, ethanol, lactate, methanol, sulfur, and hydrogen gas are suitable electron donor for perchlorate-reducing bacteria and they play important role in effective biochemical reduction of perchlorate. Microbial reduction technology may require high monitoring and maintenance and are difficult to implement in existing facility with high output. The specific issues with this treatment option include sustainability; stability; the multiple barrier concept; potential pathogen release; distribution system impacts; and utility, public, and primacy agency acceptance. These processes require the control of any potential microbial, nutrient, and electron donor releases into the distribution system.
